title: Introduction to Mathematics in Machine Learning
summary: Set up the core question and why it matters.
suggested_visuals: concept map

title: Why machine learning needs mathematics
transcript_chunk: How does a machine learning model determine whether an image contains a dog or a cat? Computers cannot process images, texts, audio or videos directly like humans. Instead, we need to translate these inputs into a language they can understand, mathematics. This translation is made possible by the foundations of linear algebra.
summary: The teacher opens with the question of how a model tells a dog from a cat, then explains that computers cannot understand raw images, text, audio, or video directly. Mathematics is introduced as the translation layer, with linear algebra as the foundation for representing and transforming data.
examples: images, text, audio, and video as data
suggested_visuals: Computer icons next to image/text/audio/video inputs
Arrow from raw data to mathematical representation

title: Data as scalars, vectors, matrices, and tensors
transcript_chunk: Linear algebra helps convert data into mathematical objects through vectorization. In this process, raw data is expressed as objects such as scalar, vector, matrix or tensors format that a computer can process and reason about. There are four fundamental data types that form the foundations of linear algebra. Scalars, vector, matrix and tensor. A scalar is the simplest unit, a single number representing a magnitude or quantity. A vector extends this idea into one dimensional sequence or list of numbers. A matrix is a two dimensional structure composed of rows and columns. A tensor generalizes this idea in even higher dimensional space.
summary: The lesson explains vectorization: turning raw data into scalar, vector, matrix, or tensor form so machines can compute with it. Each data type is defined intuitively, from a single number to multi-dimensional arrays, with images and TensorFlow used as examples.
examples: an image represented as a matrix
suggested_visuals: Four-panel diagram: scalar, vector, matrix, tensor
Image pixels shown as a matrix
3D-to-higher-dimension tensor illustration

title: Embeddings and similarity in ML
transcript_chunk: Text can be tokenized and represented as a vector or numbers that captures its semantic meaning. We can compare how similar two sentences are by calculating distance between their respective vectors or embeddings. They can be measured by a number of distance metrics such as the Euclidean distance or cosine similarity. The Euclidean distance is a numeric measure between two vectors. The cosine similarity measures the angle between the two vectors. The closer the two vectors are in their semantic meaning, the smaller the angles will be.
summary: The teacher shows how vector operations support practical ML tasks like text comparison. Sentences can be tokenized and turned into embeddings, then compared using Euclidean distance or cosine similarity. The segment explains both measures intuitively and connects vector geometry to semantic meaning.
examples: vectors at 90 degrees as independent features
suggested_visuals: Two vectors with an angle between them
Sentence embeddings plotted in space
Side-by-side Euclidean distance vs cosine similarity graphic

title: Matrix operations and neural networks
transcript_chunk: In machine learning, the vector dot product can be extended to matrix dot product. Matrix dot product is one of the most important operations in linear algebra. It is used in all kinds of neural networks ranging from simple vanilla neural net to state of the art transformer models.
summary: The lesson transitions from vector dot products to matrix dot products, emphasizing matrix multiplication as one of the most important operations in linear algebra. It is presented as essential to neural networks, from simple models to transformers.
suggested_visuals: Matrix multiplication diagram
Neural network layers connected by matrix operations

title: SVD and dimensionality reduction
transcript_chunk: Modern LLMs are trained on billions and billions of tokens and computations using all dimensions are impractical and inefficient. Instead, dimensionality reduction algorithms using linear algebra principles such as singular value decomposition or SVD allows us to perform matrix factorization, a process of breaking down a complex large matrix into smaller, more manageable and maintainable matrices such that they can be reconstructed back to form the large matrix. Using SVD, we can break down one single large matrix A into three smaller matrices U, sigma and V transposed.
summary: The teacher explains why large-scale ML computations can be impractical and introduces dimensionality reduction through singular value decomposition. SVD is described as breaking a large matrix into three smaller matrices that can later reconstruct the original, while keeping only the most informative features.
examples: large language models trained on billions of tokens
reducing a large matrix into smaller matrices
suggested_visuals: Large matrix splitting into U, Σ, V^T
Before-and-after dimensionality reduction graphic

title: SVD example with a movie-rating matrix
transcript_chunk: Let's think of A as a movie data set where it has rows and columns. So rows might represent user and the columns might represent the movies and the fields is populated with the user's rating of the movies. After vectorizing A into matrix into this large matrix, we can use SVD to break it down into three smaller matrices U sigma and V transposed. U is the left singular matrix. Sigma is a diagonal matrix. V transposed is the matrix that represents features in the column space or the movie space.
summary: The teacher uses a movie dataset to make SVD more concrete: rows represent users, columns represent movies, and ratings fill the matrix. The explanation clarifies the role of U, Sigma, and V transpose, and concludes by summarizing why linear algebra is foundational for modern machine learning.
examples: reconstructing the original matrix from smaller matrices
suggested_visuals: Movie-rating matrix with users on rows and movies on columns
Three-matrix decomposition visualization

title: Dimensionality reduction with SVD
transcript_chunk: Dimensionality reduction algorithms using linear algebra principles such as singular value decomposition or SVD allows us to perform matrix factorization, a process of breaking down a complex large matrix into smaller, more manageable and maintainable matrices such that they can be reconstructed back to form the large matrix. Using SVD, we can break down one single large matrix A into three smaller matrices U sigma and V transposed.
summary: The lesson introduces SVD as a way to break large matrices into smaller pieces, keep only the most informative parts, and reconstruct the original matrix.

title: Movie ratings and recommendation systems
transcript_chunk: Let's think of A as a movie data set where it has rows and columns. So rows might represent user and the columns might represent the movies and the fields is populated with the user's rating of the movies. After vectorizing A into matrix into this large matrix, we can use SVD to break it down into three smaller matrices U sigma and V transposed.
summary: SVD is illustrated with a movie ratings matrix, where rows represent users and columns represent movies, showing how factorization captures user and movie features.

title: Why linear algebra matters in ML systems
transcript_chunk: Linear algebra provides the essential framework that allows machine learning system to operate. It transforms raw data into structured numerical representations, enables efficient computation through matrix operations, and supports large scale optimization through modern libraries such as PyTorch and TensorFlow.
summary: The closing emphasizes that linear algebra makes computation efficient, structure manageable, and ML systems scalable across modern libraries and models.
suggested_visuals: Pipeline from raw data to optimized model
PyTorch and TensorFlow logos
